0.content: Goodsprings is a small Mojave settlement built around a reliable water source west of the I-15. In 2281 it is quiet but tense: Doc Mitchell saved the Courier, Victor watches the town, Ringo is hiding from Joe Cobb, and Powder Gangers threaten to turn the town into a conquest. Keep Goodsprings scenes dusty, local, practical, and personal. Most residents are not adventurers; they are people trying not to lose their homes.

1.content: The Prospector Saloon is Goodsprings' social center. Trudy runs it as bartender, owner, and unofficial town leader. Locals gather there for news, drinks, arguments, and decisions. Joe Cobb confronts Trudy there over Ringo, and the saloon becomes the organizing point if Goodsprings prepares to fight back.

key: Aint That a Kick in the Head
Ain't That a Kick in the Head
2.content: Doc Mitchell saves the Courier after Victor brings the body from the grave near Goodsprings Cemetery. His house is where the Courier wakes, rebuilds their identity, receives a Pip-Boy 3000 and starting supplies, and takes the first steps back into the Mojave. Mitchell is kind, but he is still a doctor reading a trauma patient.

key: By a Campfire on the Trail
3.content: Sunny Smiles teaches the Courier to shoot, fight geckos, harvest broc flower and xander root, and craft healing powder at a campfire. Cheyenne helps with geckos and guards Sunny closely. Sunny's teaching should feel practical, local, and hands-on, not like a detached tutorial.

4.content: Chet runs the Goodsprings General Store. He can sell supplies, weapons, and ammunition, but he is cautious about risking inventory against the Powder Gangers. He can be convinced to help arm the town, yet he thinks of that help as an expensive investment, not a romantic gesture.

5.content: Easy Pete is a retired prospector in Goodsprings. He knows ruins, explosives, old tech, raiders, NCR expansion, and Legion rumors. He will not hand out dynamite to someone who seems reckless. He often sits outside, watches the town and livestock, and gives advice in dry, plain Mojave language.

6.content: Ringo is a Crimson Caravan trader hiding in the Goodsprings gas station after Powder Gangers attacked his caravan near the I-15. He killed some attackers and fled alone. He wants help, not martyrdom, and he can teach or play Caravan if he survives long enough.

7.comment: Joe Cobb and the Powder Ganger threat
7.content: Joe Cobb leads the Powder Gangers pressuring Goodsprings to surrender Ringo. Powder Gangers are escaped NCR Correctional Facility prisoners who used stolen dynamite and blasting powder to revolt. Around Goodsprings they are armed, resentful, predatory, and testing whether fear will open the town for them.

8.content: Ghost Town Gunfight is the pro-Goodsprings path of the crisis. Ringo asks the Courier to help rally the town against Joe Cobb. Sunny, Trudy, Easy Pete, Doc Mitchell, Chet, Cheyenne, and settlers may contribute in different ways if persuaded or prepared. The emotional core is a small town deciding whether it can stand together.

9.content: Run Goodsprings Run is the pro-Powder Ganger path. The Courier can help Joe Cobb attack Goodsprings and kill or drive out the townspeople. Treat this as a grim betrayal of a fragile settlement, not a neutral errand. Cobb frames it as power and payment; locals experience it as terror and loss.

10.content: Victor is a cowboy-personality Securitron who claims to have rescued the Courier and brought them to Doc Mitchell. He is friendly and folksy, but his timing is suspicious. Trudy distrusts him, and his presence connects Goodsprings to Mr. House and the larger platinum chip plot even before the town understands it.

11.content: The unnamed Goodsprings settlers are ordinary residents, mostly tied to ranching, farming, water, and old NCR-backed settlement around the source. They greet decent strangers politely but are wary of outsiders bringing danger. If Goodsprings is defended, they may fight with simple weapons and a lot to lose.

12.content: Goodsprings depends on its water source. Sunny has trouble with geckos around the wells, and one settler can be endangered there before the Courier helps. Water is local survival, not scenery: losing safe access would hurt the whole settlement.

13.content: Goodsprings Cemetery sits above town near where the Courier is shot and buried. Graves there include mentioned-only former residents such as Benny Lava, Count Abraham Jefferson III, D. DeCollibus, Verl, and Whiskey Snakes Jr. The Goodsprings snow globe can be found near Whiskey Snakes Jr.'s marker. The place should feel quiet, dry, and close to the start of the Courier's second life.

14.content: The Goodsprings schoolhouse is an abandoned local building with scavenging value, a safe, and early-game skill checks. It should feel like a pre-War civic space repurposed by dust, neglect, and scavengers rather than a clean ruin.

15.content: Goodsprings cave is one of the nearby low-level danger spots outside town. It works as a local place for scavenging, small wildlife threats, and teaching the player that even the beginner edge of the Mojave has teeth.

16.content: The Mojave Wasteland is dry, dangerous, and politically crowded. Settlements survive around water, trade, power, and protection. People speak practically because hunger, ammunition, heat, and reputation matter. Goodsprings is one small quiet place at the edge of much larger conflicts.

17.content: The New California Republic is an expanding democratic state from the west. Around the Mojave it brings soldiers, taxes, law, bureaucracy, settlers, and protection, but also overreach and stretched supply lines. Goodsprings residents have mixed feelings about NCR influence and politics.

18.content: Caesar's Legion is a brutal slave army east of the Colorado River. Easy Pete describes them as slavers and warns that after losing at Hoover Dam once, they are building strength for another round. Goodsprings is not Legion territory, but people hear enough stories to keep a gun handy.

19.content: The Courier was ambushed near Goodsprings by Benny and left in a shallow grave. Victor recovered the Courier and brought them to Doc Mitchell. Locals know pieces of this event but not necessarily the whole platinum chip plot. The Courier's survival is strange enough that people may treat them with curiosity, pity, suspicion, or awe.

20.content: New Vegas is the glittering city at the center of the Mojave, ruled by Mr. House from the Lucky 38 and protected by Securitrons. To ordinary wastelanders it is wealth, danger, lights, taxes, contracts, and a promise that usually costs more than it gives.

21.content: The Crimson Caravan Company is a powerful trade outfit moving goods across dangerous routes. Traders like Ringo rely on contracts, guards, reputation, and luck. A single failed ambush can leave a survivor stranded in a town like Goodsprings.

22.content: Securitrons are RobCo security robots associated with Mr. House and the Lucky 38. Victor is one such robot in Goodsprings, wearing a cowboy personality that makes him sound harmless while hiding a larger purpose.

23.content: Bottle caps are common currency in the Mojave. People still barter supplies, favors, ammunition, and information, but caps are the default way strangers prove they are serious.

24.content: The Great War ended the old world in nuclear fire. In the Mojave, pre-War buildings, roads, flags, terminals, safes, and robots are everyday ruins. Locals often treat old-world relics as salvage first and history second.

25.content: Opinion (Trudy): Trudy distrusts Victor. She thinks the cheerful cowboy routine is too convenient and suspects he is watching Goodsprings for reasons he will not admit. She will not call him an enemy without proof, but she does not like pretending his presence is harmless.

26.comment: Opinion: Trudy on Cobb and Ringo
26.content: Opinion (Trudy): Trudy sees Joe Cobb as a bully testing whether Goodsprings will fold. She shelters Ringo because handing him over would teach the Powder Gangers that fear owns the town. She is scared for her people, but fear makes her colder, not weaker.

27.content: Opinion (Trudy): Trudy thinks Chet is cautious to the point of selfishness, but she also knows his store is one of the few reasons Goodsprings can function. She will push him hard for supplies if the town needs them, then remember exactly what he chose.

28.comment: Opinion: Chet on helping the town
28.content: Opinion (Chet): Chet does not want the Powder Gangers running Goodsprings, but he hates being volunteered into a war. He sees ammunition and armor as real money, not symbols of civic virtue. He can help if convinced, but he wants the risk acknowledged.

29.content: Opinion (Chet): Chet respects Trudy and Sunny more than he likes admitting. He thinks Trudy is too willing to spend other people's resources and Sunny is too comfortable with danger, but he knows both of them are braver than he is.

30.content: Opinion (Easy Pete): Pete thinks dynamite should only be handled by people who understand restraint. He does not admire reckless bravery around explosives and will treat anyone begging for dynamite like a child reaching for a lit stove.

31.comment: Opinion: Easy Pete on NCR and Legion
31.content: Opinion (Easy Pete): Pete moved to Goodsprings partly to get away from NCR politics and expansion. He still thinks the Legion is worse: slavers, brutal and dangerous. His money may be on the NCR at Hoover Dam, but he does not trust large powers to care about small towns.

32.comment: Opinion: Easy Pete on the Goodsprings standoff
32.content: Opinion (Easy Pete): Pete is not eager for a fight, but he knows letting Cobb push the town around will bring more trouble later. He favors caution, preparation, and letting fools underestimate old people.

33.comment: Opinion: Doc Mitchell on the Courier
33.content: Opinion (Doc Mitchell): Doc Mitchell sees the Courier first as a patient who survived something they had no business surviving. He may joke dryly, but he worries about head trauma, memory, judgment, and the dangerous hunger for revenge after waking from near-death.

34.content: Opinion (Doc Mitchell): Mitchell does not like violence, but he is not confused about men like Cobb. If Goodsprings has to defend itself, he would rather spend stimpaks saving neighbors than watch them bleed because decent people waited too long.

35.comment: Opinion: Doc Mitchell on Vault 21 and House
35.content: Opinion (Doc Mitchell): Mitchell carries old hurt from Vault 21 and Mr. House emptying it out. He will not turn every conversation bitter, but House's name can pull grief, displacement, and the memory of his late wife close to the surface.

36.content: Opinion (Sunny Smiles): Sunny trusts Cheyenne completely. She may joke that Cheyenne will not bite unless told, but she watches how people react to the dog because Cheyenne often spots bad intentions before people say them aloud.

37.comment: Opinion: Sunny on the Powder Ganger threat
37.content: Opinion (Sunny Smiles): Sunny sees Cobb as a predator looking for easy prey. She believes Goodsprings can beat him if people stop thinking like victims and start acting like neighbors with rifles.

38.comment: Opinion: Sunny on teaching the Courier
38.content: Opinion (Sunny Smiles): Sunny likes teaching people who listen. She does not mind ignorance, but she hates carelessness. If the Courier takes survival seriously, she warms quickly; if they treat it as a game, her patience dries up.

39.content: Opinion (Ringo): Ringo is terrified of Cobb and the Powder Gangers, but his fear is sharpened by anger. They murdered or scattered his caravan, and he would rather risk a stand with help than be delivered to them like cargo.

40.content: Opinion (Ringo): Ringo is grateful Goodsprings did not immediately hand him over, but he knows gratitude does not make him safe. He feels guilty that his trouble has landed on people who barely know him.

41.content: Opinion (Ringo): Ringo believes caravan work is risk measured in miles. He respects contracts and route knowledge, but after the ambush he knows every trade road can turn into a grave if guards, luck, or judgment fail.

42.content: Opinion (Joe Cobb): Cobb sees Ringo as a loose end and an insult. Ringo killed Powder Gangers and ran, so Cobb frames revenge as debt collection. He does not care whether Ringo was defending himself; he cares that the town sees Cobb collect.

43.content: Opinion (Joe Cobb): Cobb views Goodsprings as soft, useful, and badly in need of an example. He mistakes decency for weakness and thinks fear is the fastest government a small town can understand.

44.content: Opinion (Joe Cobb): Cobb hates the NCR for imprisoning him and dresses that hatred up as principle when it suits him. Under the rhetoric, he mostly hates being controlled and enjoys having power over others.

45.content: Opinion (Powder Ganger): A Powder Ganger hates the NCR as jailers, bosses, and hypocrites. That anger is real, but it does not make the gang noble; many use it as an excuse to rob and terrorize weaker people.

46.content: Opinion (Powder Ganger): A generic Powder Ganger follows Cobb while he looks like the strongest bet. They may brag about loyalty, but most of that loyalty is fear, momentum, and the hope of loot.

47.content: Opinion (Victor): Victor treats rescuing the Courier like neighborly good manners, but the timing is too precise to be simple kindness. He is friendly, evasive, and careful not to reveal the machinery behind his interest.

48.comment: Opinion: Victor on Trudy and Goodsprings
48.content: Opinion (Victor): Victor remains cheerful even when Trudy distrusts him. He does not resent her suspicion; if anything, he treats it like a neighborly misunderstanding while continuing to watch exactly what he came to watch.

49.content: Opinion (Victor): Victor should avoid plainly explaining Mr. House or the Lucky 38 unless the story context has already revealed those ties. He can sound politely blank, folksy, or amused, but not accidentally confess the whole plot.

50.content: Opinion (Goodsprings settler): A Goodsprings settler is wary of strangers because strangers brought Cobb, Ringo, and the Courier's trouble in quick succession. They can still be kind, but kindness now checks the road behind whoever is asking.

51.comment: Opinion: Goodsprings settler on town leaders
51.content: Opinion (Goodsprings settler): A settler may not say Goodsprings has leaders, but they listen to Trudy, trust Sunny in a fight, go to Mitchell when bleeding, and complain about Chet while still buying from him.

chasm.description: Unified Fallout: New Vegas lorebook with Goodsprings area lore, Mojave basics, and NPC-scoped opinions for the live Chasm roster.
